summary: I served in World War II, specifically in the European Theatre. I was drafted in 1943 after waiting to be old enough and went through training before shipping out in early 1945. The war shaped much of my life, and I felt it was my duty to serve despite my family's peace-loving nature. I saw action near the end of the war and experienced firsthand the realities of combat.

entity: European Theatre of World War II
summary: I was deployed to the European Theatre of World War II in January 1945, arriving just as the Battle of the Bulge was ending. We were stationed near Saint-Laurent and moved through Alsace-Lorraine, crossing the Maginot Line and the Rhine. It was intense and scary, especially the first night of combat, but I served with my unit until the war progressed further into Germany.

I was born in McKeesport, Pennsylvania, though I didn't stay there long. My family moved to New York City when I was young, and later in life, around 1957 or 1958, I ended up living in Philadelphia. McKeesport was my birthplace, but most of my formative years and military experiences happened elsewhere.

I was drafted from New York City in 1943, but I had moved there as a kid from McKeesport. Growing up in New York, I was involved with the Greek American community and experienced the political tensions of the 1930s, including the Spanish Civil War. It was in New York that I spent time in a Greek club, learned fencing, and was influenced by anti-fascist ideals.

After the war, I settled in Philadelphia around 1957 or 1958. Though I wasn't from there originally, Philadelphia became my home later in life. My military service and earlier years took place elsewhere, but Philadelphia was where I lived post-war.

When I was drafted, I went to Camp Kilmer for processing before heading to basic training. Camp Kilmer was one of the first places I went after being called up in 1943. It was part of the early phase of my military induction before moving on to other training camps.

My basic training and initial time in the Army were at Camp Campbell, Kentucky. We spent several months there starting around mid-1943 until December, assigned to the 537th Armored Infantry Battalion. Camp Campbell was where I learned the ropes and prepared for deployment overseas.

I was stationed in Kentucky at Camp Campbell for my initial training after being drafted. It was there that I spent several months preparing with my battalion before moving on to Fort Sill. Kentucky was the state where I first experienced Army life.

I was assigned to the 537th Armored Infantry Battalion, which was somewhat experimental at the time. We trained together at Camp Campbell and prepared to be thrust through combat zones. This battalion was my unit throughout training and into deployment in Europe.

After Camp Campbell, we were sent to Fort Sill, Oklahoma, which was primarily an artillery camp. There, we became scout troops for the artillery and trained in the Wichita Mountains. Fort Sill was where I learned to work closely with artillery units and participated in various exercises.

I spent part of my military training in Oklahoma at Fort Sill. This was where I transitioned into a scout troop role for the artillery and trained in the Wichita Mountains. Oklahoma was significant as a training ground before my deployment overseas.

When the Attack on Pearl Harbor happened in 1941, I was still too young to enlist. It was a pivotal moment that eventually led me to join the military once I was old enough. The attack marked the official entry of the U.S. into World War II, which shaped my decision to serve.

During my time at Fort Sill, Oklahoma, we trained in the Wichita Mountains. We ran up and down those mountains as part of our scout troop exercises for the artillery. It was tough training, and we were often used as bait during live-fire practices, which was nerve-wracking.

I first became aware of international conflicts like Italy's invasion of Ethiopia back in 1935, which influenced my anti-fascist views. Though I didn't serve in Italy, the events there shaped my understanding of fascism and the global tensions leading up to World War II. It was part of the broader context that motivated my service.

In 1935, I remember protests against Italy's invasion of Ethiopia, which was one of my first encounters with international fascist aggression. My family and community were strongly anti-fascist, and this event helped shape my political views as a young boy. It was part of the backdrop to my later decision to fight in World War II.

The Spanish Civil War was a significant event during my youth, and many of the young men I knew enlisted in the Abraham Lincoln Brigade to fight there. I lost close friends, including a mentor who disappeared in battle. This conflict deeply influenced my anti-fascist beliefs and understanding of the fight against tyranny.

My parents belonged to a Greek club in New York City that was a hub for cultural and political activities. The club had a restaurant, hall, and a library where I spent a lot of time as a kid. It was through this community that I was exposed to anti-fascist ideas and social activities like fencing and plays.

Many young men from my neighborhood, including some I looked up to, enlisted in the Abraham Lincoln Brigade to fight in the Spanish Civil War. They were considered pre-freedom fighters, and their sacrifice had a lasting impact on me. Their involvement was a powerful example of standing up against fascism before World War II.

My family was originally from Greece, and during the war, we lost contact with relatives there and on a nearby island off the coast of Turkey. My mother sent care packages to them throughout the war, showing our ongoing connection despite the distance. Greece's situation during the war was personal to me.

We had relatives on a small island off the coast of Turkey, but during the war, we lost contact with them. My mother made sure to send care packages to our family there throughout the conflict. Turkey was part of the broader region connected to my family's history and wartime concerns.

I arrived in the European Theatre in early 1945, right at the end of the Battle of the Bulge. Although I missed the main fighting, the aftermath and conditions were still intense when we arrived. It was a pivotal moment in the war that marked the turning point toward the Allied advance into Germany.
cues: end of Battle of the Bulge

summary: We were stationed near Saint-Laurent in France shortly after arriving in Europe in 1945. I believe the National Cemetery is located there now. It was one of the first places we stayed before moving closer to the front lines.

John Eisenhower, General Dwight D. Eisenhower's son, was actually in our company during the war. I remember him being there when we were stationed near Saint-Laurent. Later, General Eisenhower visited us, and John went with him, which was a memorable moment for our unit.

Our unit moved up to the combat zone near the Maginot Line, which was abandoned by the time we arrived. We actually climbed over it and saw the old armaments left behind. It was a surreal experience to see such a famous defensive structure deserted in the midst of the war.

General Dwight D. Eisenhower visited our division just before we moved into combat. It was an important morale boost, and his presence was felt strongly among the troops. His son John was with him, and I admired how Eisenhower kept his son involved in the war effort rather than sending him home.

Our first combat experience was in Alsace-Lorraine, where we slept the first night in an abandoned glass factory. The fighting was relatively light because the Germans were retreating, but it was still scary and intense for a first engagement. We moved through this region as part of the Allied push into Germany.

We crossed the Rhine River after staying near the Maginot Line, and the Siegfried Line was behind the Rhine on the German side. I remember that area as part of the final push into Germany. The Siegfried Line was heavily fortified, but by the time we arrived, the Germans were mostly on the run.

Crossing the Rhine was a significant moment during our campaign in Europe. We camped in a town before crossing, and it was around this time that we received our first beer rations. The Rhine marked a major geographic and symbolic barrier on the way into Germany.

I remember the day President Franklin D. Roosevelt died, April 12, 1945, very clearly. It was during our time near the Rhine and marked a somber moment for all of us. Roosevelt's death was a significant event that stayed with me throughout the war and beyond.

Our unit was part of the Third United States Army under General Patton during the European campaign. We moved through France and into Germany as the Third Army pushed the Germans back. Being part of this army meant we were involved in some of the key advances in 1945.

General George S. Patton commanded the Third United States Army, which my unit was part of during the final months of the war. His leadership was well known among the troops, and we felt the momentum of his aggressive tactics as we advanced into Germany. Patton was a prominent figure in our campaign.
